text:
Thus, like kin finding one another in the dark,
We spoke across the chamber walls,
Until the creeping moss reached our mouths
And blurred the inscriptions of our names.
source_text:
And so, as kinsmen met a night,
We talked between the rooms,
Until the moss had reached our lips,
And covered up our names.

text:
I saw the earth was choked with burial plots,
Cold monuments standing where the flora should have thrived;
Clerics in dark robes paced their constant circuits,
Lashing my every joy and longing with jagged thorns.
source_text:
And I saw it was filled with graves,
And tombstones where flowers should be;
And priests in black gowns were walking their rounds,
And binding with briars my joys and desires.

text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
What are the stakes? I am certain of the prize—
provided You grant me the liberty
to draw him quietly toward my own way.
source_text:
MEPHISTOPHELES:
What will You bet?—now am sure of winning—
Only, observe You give me full permission
To lead him softly on my path.

text:
Until the earth is bolted shut,
This joy defies all measure;
For though they seal you in the dark,
I keep the key myself.
source_text:
Until they lock it in the grave,
'T is bliss I cannot weigh,
For though they lock thee in the grave,
Myself can hold the key.

text: Line 14’s "sunburnt mirth" demonstrates Keats’s remarkable ability to condense meaning. While the text contains no direct reference to any individuals, the phrase evokes a vivid image of joyful, bronzed peasants as effectively as any lengthy or complex characterization.
source_text:
l. 14. sunburnt mirth. An instance of Keats's power of concentration.
The people are not mentioned at all, yet this phrase conjures up a
picture of merry, laughing, sunburnt peasants, as surely as could a long
and elaborate description.

text:
Towering figures, black and vast,
Seemed to wheel through the air above,
Their shrieks dissolving into the gale;
Upon the sunless wall,
Faint silhouettes flickered and fell
As he pressed forward, consumed by dread.
source_text:
And forms, dark and high,
Seemed around him to fly,
And mingle their yells with the blast:
And on the dark wall
Half-seen shadows did fall,
As enhorrored he onward passed.

text:
A sudden rift broke through my consciousness,
As if my brain had sheared in two;
I tried to bind the jagged edges,
But they would not align.
source_text:
I felt a clearing in my mind
As if my brain had split;
I tried to match it, seam by seam,
But could not make them fit.

text:
The war’s grim report,
The tribal snare, the tactical guile, the lethal landscape,
Cavalry units holding their ground with unyielding valor,
Encircled in a desperate ring, shielded by the carcasses of their mounts,
The demise of Custer, his officers, and every soldier.
source_text:
The battle-bulletin,
The Indian ambuscade, the craft, the fatal environment,
The cavalry companies fighting to the last in sternest heroism,
In the midst of their little circle, with their slaughter’d horses
for breastworks,
The fall of Custer and all his officers and men.

text:
The slightest gown will fit me,
A fragment of a crown;
For we care little for our clothes
When we are bound for home.
source_text:
The smallest "robe" will fit me,
And just a bit of "crown;"
For you know we do not mind our dress
When we are going home.

text: PAGE 62, l. 209. "their . . . man." See the note for l. 174. Observe the remarkable visual clarity of this depiction—the tranquil countryside setting and the encroachment of human emotion, as the transparent water reflects the pallid assassins, consumed by apprehension, alongside the oblivious victim, who is brimming with radiant vitality.
source_text:
PAGE 62. l. 209. their . . . man. Cf. l. 174, note. Notice the
extraordinary vividness of the picture here--the quiet rural scene and
the intrusion of human passion with the reflection in the clear water of
the pale murderers, sick with suspense, and the unsuspecting victim,
full of glowing life.

text:
I, who so recently did stray,
And shifted loves with every moon,
Little believed the approaching day
Would make me pay for regret so soon.
source_text:
“I wha sae late did range and rove,
And chang’d with every moon my love,
I little thought the time was near,
Repentance I should buy sae dear.

text:
If I could choose to catalog the masters,
To render their noble likenesses and mimic their craft at will—
Homer with his endless sieges, his Hector, Achilles, and Ajax,
Or the shadow-bound Hamlet, Lear, and Othello—even Tennyson’s elegant women—
The finest wit, the sharpest meter, or the perfect rhyme that singers crave;
All these, O Sea, I would gladly trade away
If you would grant me the pulse of a single wave, its secret motion,
Or exhale your salt-heavy breath across my lines
And leave your scent lingering there.
source_text:
Had I the choice to tally greatest bards,
To limn their portraits, stately, beautiful, and emulate at will,
Homer with all his wars and warriors--Hector, Achilles, Ajax,
Or Shakspere’s woe-entangled Hamlet, Lear, Othello--Tennyson’s fair ladies,
Metre or wit the best, or choice conceit to wield in perfect rhyme,
delight of singers;
These, these, O sea, all these I’d gladly barter,
Would you the undulation of one wave, its trick to me transfer,
Or breathe one breath of yours upon my verse,
And leave its odor there.

text:
Braehead’s master has quickened his stride,
For more than a year or two, man;
The Lord of the Ford will be laid on his side,
If he cannot win her at all, man.
source_text:
The Laird o’ Braehead has been on his speed,
For mair than a towmond or twa, man;
The Laird o’ the Ford will straught on a board,
If he canna get her at a’, man.

text:
A shelter stands to parry storms
And hide the gentry’s face;
There Racer Jess and loose-lived girls
Peer from the entry space.
A row of whispering wenches sit
With bosoms bared to view;
While weaver youths from Kilmarnock
Their ruffian jests pursue,
For sport this day.
source_text:
Here stands a shed to fend the show’rs,
An’ screen our countra gentry;
There Racer Jess, an’ twa-three whores,
Are blinkin at the entry.
Here sits a raw o’ tittlin jads,
Wi’ heaving breast an’ bare neck;
An’ there a batch o’ wabster lads,
Blackguarding frae Kilmarnock,
For fun this day.

text:
A way to India!
Look, my spirit—can you not see the original intent?
The world bridged, laced with a living grid,
Peoples made neighbors, joined in the rites of union,
The deep seas crossed, the remote drawn into reach,
The very continents forged together.
source_text:
Passage to India!
Lo, soul, seest thou not God’s purpose from the first?
The earth to be spann’d, connected by network,
The races, neighbors, to marry and be given in marriage,
The oceans to be cross’d, the distant brought near,
The lands to be welded together.

text:
As Phoebus crowns the dawn with gold,
Dispelling every murky shade,
While nature smiles, in beauty dressed;
So shines for me my lovely maid.
When from my Chloris I am torn,
Despondent, grieved, and all forlorn,
The murky clouds of night descend to veil my sky;
But when her grace enchants my gaze,
In all her beauty’s brilliant blaze—
When deep into my heart
Her glowing splendors dart;
’Tis then—’tis then I wake to life and joy!
source_text:
Phoebus gilding the brow of morning,
Banishes ilk darksome shade,
Nature, gladdening and adorning;
Such to me my lovely maid.
When frae my Chloris parted,
Sad, cheerless, broken-hearted,
The night’s gloomy shades, cloudy, dark, o’ercast my sky:
But when she charms my sight,
In pride of Beauty’s light—
When thro’ my very heart
Her burning glories dart;
’Tis then—’tis then I wake to life and joy!

text:
The aspirations that swell in young chests
Cannot survive the attrition of years.
Love’s blossom is hemmed in by spines;
The atmosphere is harsh and biting
Where its honors take root.
Youth claims, “The violet petals are mine,”
Though they perish even as they shine.
source_text:
Hopes, that swell in youthful breasts,
Live not through the waste of time!
Love’s rose a host of thorns invests;
Cold, ungenial is the clime,
Where its honours blow.
Youth says, ‘The purple flowers are mine,’
Which die the while they glow.

text:
O Virtue, you ruinous blight,
Vast multitudes you’ve robbed of light!
Empty is the hope of him who relies
On moral mercy, truth, and where justice lies!
source_text:
Morality, thou deadly bane,
Thy tens o’ thousands thou hast slain!
Vain is his hope, whase stay an’ trust is
In moral mercy, truth, and justice!

text:
Next, many a surgeon’s tools and blades,
Of every form and metal grades,
With jars and flasks of various shades,
He’s sure to own;
Their Latin names he glibly trades
As ABCs are known.
source_text:
“And then, a’ doctor’s saws an’ whittles,
Of a’ dimensions, shapes, an’ mettles,
A’ kind o’ boxes, mugs, an’ bottles,
He’s sure to hae;
Their Latin names as fast he rattles
as A B C.

text:
When autumn’s middle days reach twilight’s glow,
A distant chill foretells the winter’s breath;
The weary west is robbed of golden show,
While through the leaves resounds a dance of death.
The woods are stripped and left in nakedness,
Before he dares to leave his northern cave
And wander far. So lovely Isabel
By slow decline from her own beauty fell.
source_text:
In the mid days of autumn, on their eves
The breath of Winter comes from far away,
And the sick west continually bereaves
Of some gold tinge, and plays a roundelay
Of death among the bushes and the leaves,
To make all bare before he dares to stray
From his north cavern. So sweet Isabel
By gradual decay from beauty fell,

text:
Still, they managed to pilfer the basil-jar,
To scrutinize it in a hidden nook:
The object was foul, stained with sickly hues,
Yet they recognized Lorenzo’s visage there:
The prize of their dark homicide was won,
And so they fled from Florence instantly,
Never to return.—They hurried forth,
To exile, with the weight of blood upon them.
source_text:
Yet they contriv'd to steal the Basil-pot,
And to examine it in secret place:
The thing was vile with green and livid spot,
And yet they knew it was Lorenzo's face:
The guerdon of their murder they had got,
And so left Florence in a moment's space,
Never to turn again.--Away they went,
With blood upon their heads, to banishment.

text:
There are moments I sit, spirit sagging,
Passing long, restless intervals of dread;
I can find no joy in the open book,
Nor rest within this arbor of lessons,
Exhausted by the bleak, relentless downpour.
source_text:
Ah then at times I drooping sit,
And spend many an anxious hour;
Nor in my book can I take delight,
Nor sit in learning’s bower,
Worn through with the dreary shower.

text: Line 21: "She" represents Melancholy, here elevated to the status of a goddess. This conceptualization should be compared with the passage in *Lamia*, i. 190-200. Reference may also be made to Milton’s personifications of Melancholy within *L'Allegro* and *Il Penseroso*.
source_text:
l. 21. She, i.e. Melancholy--now personified as a goddess. Compare
this conception of melancholy with the passage in Lamia, i. 190-200.
Cf. also Milton's personifications of Melancholy in L'Allegro and _Il
Penseroso_.

text:
The Divine shaped a modest gentian;
It strove to be a rose
And failed, as the bright months sneered.
But on the edge of the winter,
A violet spirit emerged
To captivate the whole hillside;
Summer bowed her head in shame,
And the taunting fell silent.
The frost was its necessity;
That royal pigment refused to rise
Until the North Wind summoned it.
"Maker! Shall I open now?"
source_text:
God made a little gentian;
It tried to be a rose
And failed, and all the summer laughed.
But just before the snows
There came a purple creature
That ravished all the hill;
And summer hid her forehead,
And mockery was still.
The frosts were her condition;
The Tyrian would not come
Until the North evoked it.
"Creator! shall I bloom?"

text:
The pale specter of mortality has at last
Receded from my celestial orbit,
That suffocating veil of ice and heavy sleep.
Now, through my freshly woven sanctuaries,
Radiant lovers drift,
Diminished in stature, yet as tender as those
Who dwell within your deepest vales.
source_text:
THE MOON:
The shadow of white death has passed
From my path in heaven at last,
A clinging shroud of solid frost and sleep;
And through my newly-woven bowers,
Wander happy paramours,
Less mighty, but as mild as those who keep
Thy vales more deep.

text:
The vanishing spectacle recedes,
The core of a maker’s spirit or a sage’s lifelong inquiry,
The grueling labors of the soldier, the martyr, and the brave,
To shape a phantom likeness of the soul.
source_text:
The ostent evanescent,
The substance of an artist’s mood or savan’s studies long,
Or warrior’s, martyr’s, hero’s toils,
To fashion his eidolon.

text:
The soil keeps no secrets; its abundance is more than enough.
The world’s realities linger in the open, hardly hidden at all.
They are hushed and intricate, impossible to bind in ink.
They soak through every fiber, surrendering themselves freely.
Extending a feeling and a calling, I articulate them over and over.
I am silent, yet if you fail to hear me, what purpose do I serve?
To sustain, to elevate—without these, what is my worth to you?
source_text:
The earth does not withhold, it is generous enough,
The truths of the earth continually wait, they are not so conceal’d either,
They are calm, subtle, untransmissible by print,
They are imbued through all things conveying themselves willingly,
Conveying a sentiment and invitation, I utter and utter,
I speak not, yet if you hear me not of what avail am I to you?
To bear, to better, lacking these of what avail am I?

text:
Three high-spirited friends, with hearts free of stain,
Craigdarroch, where wit, worth, and legal skill reign;
Trusty Glenriddel, who knows every ancient-mint coin,
And brave Robert, whom cellars of vintage conjoin.

Cythna’s voice held the echo of distant, murmuring water,
rising and falling as our words tangled in the storm.
We sat together until the conversation turned
to the sudden, jagged wreck of what had been,
and how we might yet plant the seeds of a hope
whose fruit is a mortal poison to all evil.
For us, this ruin became a solitary watchtower,
but her eyes were clouding with weariness;
two days had already slipped away.
source_text:
The tones of Cythna’s voice like echoes were
Of those far murmuring streams; they rose and fell,
Mixed with mine own in the tempestuous air,—
And so we sate, until our talk befell
Of the late ruin, swift and horrible,
And how those seeds of hope might yet be sown,
Whose fruit is evil’s mortal poison: well,
For us, this ruin made a watch-tower lone,
But Cythna’s eyes looked faint, and now two days were gone

text:
Goodbye to the emerald pastures and joyful woods,
Where grazing herds have found their peace;
Where lambs once fed, now softly tread
The radiant feet of spirits;
Unseen, they spill their grace
In a tide of ceaseless joy,
Over every budding flower
And every chest stilled in sleep.
source_text:
Farewell, green fields and happy groves,
Where flocks have took delight,
Where lambs have nibbled, silent moves
The feet of angels bright;
Unseen, they pour blessing,
And joy without ceasing,
On each bud and blossom,
And each sleeping bosom.

text:
Faintly she chuckles 'neath the listless moon,
As Porphyro observes her withered face,
Much like a baffled child before a beldam
Who guards a volume of dark mysteries,
Bespectacled within the chimney’s nook.
But soon his eyes grew bright, once she revealed
His lady’s plan; he scarce could hold back tears
To think of those frigid enchantments there,
And Madeline, deep in the slumber of ancient lore.
source_text:
Feebly she laugheth in the languid moon,
While Porphyro upon her face doth look,
Like puzzled urchin on an aged crone
Who keepeth clos'd a wond'rous riddle-book,
As spectacled she sits in chimney nook.
But soon his eyes grew brilliant, when she told
His lady's purpose; and he scarce could brook
Tears, at the thought of those enchantments cold
And Madeline asleep in lap of legends old.

text:
And should I, when the morning breaks,
It’s as if a hundred drums
Are thundering round my bed,
With cheers that flood my youthful sky,
And bells that ring out "triumph"
From the steeples of my soul!
source_text:
And if I do, when morning comes,
It is as if a hundred drums
Did round my pillow roll,
And shouts fill all my childish sky,
And bells keep saying 'victory'
From steeples in my soul!

text:
Deep in this fancy mused the pensive maid:
The music, mourning like a god in woe,
She barely heard; her saintly eyes, downplayed
And fixed upon the floor, saw garments flow
In passing—yet she never watched them go:
In vain did many a longing suitor near,
Then slip away; not chilled by pride’s cold show,
But simply unobserved: her heart was elsewhere,
Pining for Agnes’ dreams, the sweetest of the year.
source_text:
Full of this whim was thoughtful Madeline:
The music, yearning like a God in pain,
She scarcely heard: her maiden eyes divine,
Fix'd on the floor, saw many a sweeping train
Pass by--she heeded not at all: in vain
Came many a tiptoe, amorous cavalier,
And back retir'd; not cool'd by high disdain,
But she saw not: her heart was otherwhere:
She sigh'd for Agnes' dreams, the sweetest of the year.

text:
He beams at you, at me, at every soul,
The one who took a tiny, human frame;
A baby’s grin is his own smile expressed,
Charming both worlds into a state of rest.

Would that my eyes were rushing streams!
    My voice, a lioness whose screams
    Lament her young’s disaster!
    That I might weep, that I might wail,
    As Tories flee, as Tories fail,
    With fierce Whigs chasing faster!
source_text:
O that my een were flowing burns!
My voice, a lioness that mourns
Her darling cubs’ undoing!
That I might greet, that I might cry,
While Tories fall, while Tories fly,
And furious Whigs pursuing!

text:
Warble, kind thrush, upon the naked bough,
Warble, dear bird; I harken to your strain;
See ancient Winter, in his sullen reign,
At your bright song, unbend his wrinkled brow.
source_text:
Sing on, sweet thrush, upon the leafless bough,
Sing on, sweet bird, I listen to thy strain,
See aged Winter, ’mid his surly reign,
At thy blythe carol, clears his furrowed brow.

text:
In solitude she grieves for joys denied;
Her bitter tears fell till the night came on,
And then, with love no longer at her side,
She nursed her lonely ache, now he was gone.
His phantom in the gloom she seemed to see,
And to the silence made a tender moan,
Reaching her flawless arms out longingly,
Upon her bed she sighed, "Where? O where?"
source_text:
She weeps alone for pleasures not to be;
Sorely she wept until the night came on,
And then, instead of love, O misery!
She brooded o'er the luxury alone:
His image in the dusk she seem'd to see,
And to the silence made a gentle moan,
Spreading her perfect arms upon the air,
And on her couch low murmuring "Where? O where?"

text:
SEMICHORUS 1:
Where the crags that rim the Aegean
Resound with the liberation anthems
Of the brave—
I would soar,
A storm-driven harbinger of triumph!
My gilded weeping
For the Grecian slain
Would dissolve into the blood-stained surge,
And my grave, percussive thunder
Would toll for all the world to hear
The death-knell of oppression.
source_text:
SEMICHORUS 1:
Where the rocks that gird th’ Aegean
Echo to the battle paean
Of the free—
I would flee
A tempestuous herald of victory!
My golden rain
For the Grecian slain
Should mingle in tears with the bloody main,
And my solemn thunder-knell
Should ring to the world the passing-bell
Of Tyranny!

text:
Does that shuddering wail pass for music?
Could it truly be a chorus of delight?
With so many young lives left in ruin?
This is a nation defined by its want!
source_text:
Is that trembling cry a song?
Can it be a song of joy?
And so many children poor?
It is a land of poverty!

text:
The mature, the adolescent, and the newborn—of this land and every land—
sheltered or exposed, I witness them with an equal eye,
and the whole of existence behind or through them.
source_text:
Grown, half-grown and babe, of this country and every country,
in-doors and out-doors, one just as much as the other, I see,
And all else behind or through them.

text:
Ona, so bloodless and frail,
Give your father a word!
Oh, the shuddering fright,
The bleak, heavy grief
That rattles the silver blooms of my hair!
source_text:
Ona, pale and weak,
To thy father speak!
O the trembling fear!
O the dismal care
That shakes the blossoms of my hoary hair!’

text:
No flighty, maudlin signs appear
In simple faces, plain and clear!
Through trilling songs that charm the ear
You do not stray;
But deep and somber bass we hear
You hum away.
source_text:
Nae hair-brain’d, sentimental traces
In your unletter’d, nameless faces!
In arioso trills and graces
Ye never stray;
But gravissimo, solemn basses
Ye hum away.

text:
A gale arrived like a trumpet’s call;
It shuddered through the fields,
And a verdant shiver through the heat
Passed with such a dark portending
We bolted every door and pane
Against a ghost of emerald light;
The electric serpent of our doom
Slithered by in that same pulse.
Upon a frantic crowd of gasping trees,
While fences seemed to flee,
And houses ran like river-floods
The living turned to see.
The bell within the wild spire
Hurled its news into the storm.
How much can arrive
And much can depart,
Yet the world remains whole!
source_text:
There came a wind like a bugle;
It quivered through the grass,
And a green chill upon the heat
So ominous did pass
We barred the windows and the doors
As from an emerald ghost;
The doom's electric moccason
That very instant passed.
On a strange mob of panting trees,
And fences fled away,
And rivers where the houses ran
The living looked that day.
The bell within the steeple wild
The flying tidings whirled.
How much can come
And much can go,
And yet abide the world!

text:
At last, the silvered celebration broke,
In feathered caps and crowns of bright design,
As thick as phantom forms that softly woke
In young, fresh minds where olden legends shine
With gallant deeds. Let these we now decline,
To watch one Maiden with a steadfast gaze,
Who through the winter's chill did long incline
Toward love and Agnes' holy, winged ways,
As she from aged matrons heard through all her girlhood days.
source_text:
At length burst in the argent revelry,
With plume, tiara, and all rich array,
Numerous as shadows haunting fairily
The brain, new stuff'd, in youth, with triumphs gay
Of old romance. These let us wish away,
And turn, sole-thoughted, to one Lady there,
Whose heart had brooded, all that wintry day,
On love, and wing'd St. Agnes' saintly care,
As she had heard old dames full many times declare.
